segments.text: With the increased use of generative AI and the potential for increased misinformation and decreased transparency in the research space, the Institute for Public Relations (IPR) has created a disclosure policy for labeling generative AI use in research-related content.
Content labels help prevent disinformation, allow transparency for material sources, and help prevent plagiarism.
This ensures openness, accountability, honesty, and rigor of the material that IPR creates and publishes.
Generative AI cannot verify the quality or accuracy of the work it draws upon and is prone to hallucinations (or making up content).
Generative AI output may contain biased information.
Generative AI stops collecting information at a certain period of time, so it does not use the most updated research or material.

segments.B_label: restricted or prohibited uses of AI
segments.text: Confidential or proprietary information should not be uploaded or fed to generative AI (including the responses of participants who have requested confidentiality or anonymity).
Therefore, IPR requires in-text disclosures of substantive generative AI use in its research-related materials.
The research materials that require disclosure include:
Blogs, blurbs, and research summaries
IPR Research Letter articles
IPR Signature Studies
Public Relations Journal articles
IPR Deconstructing series articles
Presentation materials at IPR events or programs
Authors must disclose how generative AI was used and to what extent.

segments.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
segments.text: Generative AI should be used primarily for editorial assistance.

segments.B_label: restricted or prohibited uses of AI
segments.text: AI should not be used as a co-author for a research paper, as it cannot take responsibility for the work.

Authors and creators must take full responsibility for all content created by generative AI, including the use of copyrighted material, and ensure the content is factual, credible, accurate, and supported by other reliable sources.

segments.B_label: restricted or prohibited uses of AI
segments.text: AI should not be used as a primary or secondary source.
Instead, it is the author’s responsibility to track original content sources for proper attribution.
Each use of generative AI within a document should be addressed individually.
Authors are responsible for complying with relevant laws and regulations related to AI-generated content.

segments.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
segments.text: Generative AI use should be labeled or disclosed when used for:

segments.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
segments.text: The research process, such as collecting or analyzing the data

segments.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
segments.text: New content generation (visual or written)

segments.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
segments.text: Content a reader would assume to be human-created

segments.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
segments.text: Language translation (content should also be checked by a fluent speaker)

segments.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
segments.text: Editing that changes the style, voice, or composition of the writing

segments.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
segments.text: Generative AI typically does not require a label or disclosure for:

segments.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
segments.text: Idea or topic generation/brainstorming

segments.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
segments.text: Grammatical changes or other minor edits that do not change the overall content, style, or voice of the piece

segments.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
segments.text: Summarizing of material if it does not change the content
Ways to disclose:
In-text citation using APA style
A reference in a paragraph in the report
Footnote
Endnote
What should be included in the label or disclosure:
The generative AI program used (e.g., ChatGPT 4.0)
The prompt used (e.g., summarize these research findings in two paragraphs)
The section where generative AI was applied (e.g., introduction)
Who used the generative AI (e.g., Dr. Tina McCorkindale)
The date, if applicable

Here is an example of a footnote:
The introduction was created by inserting the bulk of the report written solely by Dr. McCorkindale, the primary author of this report, into ChatGPT 4 and then asking it to create two introductory paragraphs. Dr. McCorkindale checked the accuracy of the content and edited the introduction.
For more information about content disclosure, please refer to Dr. Cayce Myers’s IPR article, “To disclose or not to disclose: That is the AI question.”
IPR suggests that if in doubt whether AI-generated content should be labeled, it’s best to over-disclose rather than under-disclose.
Please note: This policy will evolve to ensure it remains relevant and effective throughout changes in the AI disclosure landscape.
. considerations when using generative ai

rules and guidelines for ai use in ipr-published work

projection.text:
# Institute for Public Relations
# Source: https://instituteforpr.org/ipr-ai-disclosure-policy/
# Date: 2026-01-05
# Category: guideline

IPR AI Disclosure and Labeling Policy

Posted on March 10, 2024 by Institute for Public Relations
Updated January 5, 2026

Thanks to the IPR Digital Media Research Center team for their considerations and input.

With the increased use of generative AI and the potential for increased misinformation and decreased transparency in the research space, the Institute for Public Relations (IPR) has created a disclosure policy for labeling generative AI use in research-related content.
Content labels help prevent disinformation, allow transparency for material sources, and help prevent plagiarism.
This ensures openness, accountability, honesty, and rigor of the material that IPR creates and publishes.
Generative AI cannot verify the quality or accuracy of the work it draws upon and is prone to hallucinations (or making up content).
Generative AI output may contain biased information.
Generative AI stops collecting information at a certain period of time, so it does not use the most updated research or material.
Confidential or proprietary information should not be uploaded or fed to generative AI (including the responses of participants who have requested confidentiality or anonymity).
projection.B_label: restricted or prohibited uses of AI

projection.text: Therefore, IPR requires in-text disclosures of substantive generative AI use in its research-related materials.
The research materials that require disclosure include:
Presentation materials at IPR events or programs

Rules and Guidelines for AI Use in IPR-Published Work
-
Authors must disclose how generative AI was used and to what extent.
Generative AI should be used primarily for editorial assistance.
projection.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI

projection.text: AI should not be used as a co-author for a research paper, as it cannot take responsibility for the work.
projection.B_label: restricted or prohibited uses of AI

projection.text: Authors and creators must take full responsibility for all content created by generative AI, including the use of copyrighted material, and ensure the content is factual, credible, accurate, and supported by other reliable sources.
AI should not be used as a primary or secondary source.
projection.B_label: restricted or prohibited uses of AI

projection.text: Instead, it is the author’s responsibility to track original content sources for proper attribution.
Each use of generative AI within a document should be addressed individually.
Authors are responsible for complying with relevant laws and regulations related to AI-generated content.
Generative AI use should be labeled or disclosed when used for:
projection.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI

projection.text: The research process, such as collecting or analyzing the data
projection.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
permitted or encouraged uses of AI

projection.text: Content a reader would assume to be human-created
projection.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI

projection.text: Language translation (content should also be checked by a fluent speaker)
projection.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI

projection.text: Editing that changes the style, voice, or composition of the writing
projection.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI

projection.text: Generative AI typically does not require a label or disclosure for:
projection.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI
permitted or encouraged uses of AI

projection.text: Grammatical changes or other minor edits that do not change the overall content, style, or voice of the piece
projection.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI

projection.text: Summarizing of material if it does not change the content
projection.B_label: permitted or encouraged uses of AI

projection.text: A reference in a paragraph in the report
What should be included in the label or disclosure:
The generative AI program used (e.g., ChatGPT 4.0)
The prompt used (e.g., summarize these research findings in two paragraphs)
The section where generative AI was applied (e.g., introduction)
Who used the generative AI (e.g., Dr. Tina McCorkindale)

Here is an example of a footnote:
The introduction was created by inserting the bulk of the report written solely by Dr. McCorkindale, the primary author of this report, into ChatGPT 4 and then asking it to create two introductory paragraphs. Dr. McCorkindale checked the accuracy of the content and edited the introduction.
For more information about content disclosure, please refer to Dr. Cayce Myers’s IPR article, “To disclose or not to disclose: That is the AI question.”
IPR suggests that if in doubt whether AI-generated content should be labeled, it’s best to over-disclose rather than under-disclose.
Please note: This policy will evolve to ensure it remains relevant and effective throughout changes in the AI disclosure landscape.
